subsystem: rsyslog legacy (RFC 3164 / BSD) syslog message parser. This is the function parseLegacySyslogMsg(), which takes a received syslog message object (msg_t) and splits its raw text into TIMESTAMP, HOSTNAME, TAG and MSG components, copying HOSTNAME and TAG into fixed-size stack buffers and then registering the parsed fields back on the message via MsgSetHOSTNAME/MsgSetTAG/MsgSetMSGoffs.
provenance: Identified from rsyslog-specific idioms: the msg_t type and pMsg->pszRawMsg / iLenRawMsg / offAfterPRI / tTIMESTAMP / tRcvdAt fields; the datetime.ParseTIMESTAMP3339 / ParseTIMESTAMP3164 object-method calls returning RS_RET_OK; the BEGINfunc/ENDfunc and DBGPRINTF debug macros; the CONF_TAG_MAXSIZE / CONF_HOSTNAME_MAXSIZE size macros; the flag names IGNDATE / INTERNAL_MSG / PARSE_HOSTNAME and the global bParseHOSTNAMEandTAG; and the inline attribution comments signed 'rgerhards' (Rainer Gerhards, rsyslog's author). The sample banner (primevul_c_train_3214_fixed) and the parseLegacySyslogMsg name match rsyslog tools/syslogd.c / runtime/msg.c.
trust_boundary: pMsg->pszRawMsg holds a syslog datagram received from the network (UDP/TCP port 514) or a local transport; it is fully attacker-controlled wire data. iLenRawMsg is its length and offAfterPRI is the number of leading PRI characters already consumed by an earlier stage. Everything the parser walks after offAfterPRI -- whether a timestamp is present, how long the hostname is, how long the TAG is, whether a ':' terminator appears, and whether the bytes are NUL-terminated -- is chosen by the attacker. The fixed stack buffers bufParseTAG and bufParseHOSTNAME are the destinations that untrusted, unbounded input is copied into, so the length arithmetic that bounds those copies is the central security surface.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The function receives a message object pMsg and a bitmask flags controlling how much of the message to parse. It declares p2parse, a cursor into the raw message text, and lenMsg, a signed running count of the bytes still available to parse. i is a general index. The two critical declarations are bufParseTAG, a fixed-size stack array sized by the macro CONF_TAG_MAXSIZE, and bufParseHOSTNAME, sized by CONF_HOSTNAME_MAXSIZE. These are the destination buffers into which attacker-supplied HOSTNAME and TAG bytes are copied, so their capacity and the loop bounds that write into them determine memory safety. BEGINfunc is an rsyslog tracing macro.

assert() only checks the pointers are non-NULL, and only in debug builds. lenMsg is computed as the raw length minus the count of PRI characters already consumed, i.e. the number of text bytes remaining after the priority header. p2parse is set to point just past the PRI, at the start of the parseable text. Both derive directly from attacker-influenced fields: iLenRawMsg is the received length and offAfterPRI is the result of the earlier PRI scan. The invariant the rest of the function silently assumes is offAfterPRI <= iLenRawMsg (so lenMsg >= 0) and that p2parse..p2parse+lenMsg stays inside the pszRawMsg allocation.

The parser tries to extract a timestamp. It first attempts the RFC 3339 (high precision) format via ParseTIMESTAMP3339, which is passed &p2parse and &lenMsg so it can advance the cursor and shrink the remaining count on success. If that fails it tries the RFC 3164 (legacy) format. If both fail it handles a known-malformed variant: if the first byte is a space and more than one byte remains, it steps over the space (++p2parse, --lenMsg) and retries the 3164 parse, restoring the cursor/length if that also fails. Note that on the third branch the byte *p2parse is dereferenced as the FIRST operand of the &&, before lenMsg > 1 is evaluated, so the length has not yet been confirmed positive at the point of the read.

If the caller set the IGNDATE flag, the parsed message timestamp is discarded and replaced with the reception time via a fixed-size memcpy of sizeof(struct syslogTime). Source and destination are both members of the same message object and the copy length is a compile-time sizeof, so this copy is self-bounded and not attacker-influenced.

HOSTNAME and TAG parsing only runs when the global bParseHOSTNAMEandTAG is enabled and the message is not internally generated (INTERNAL_MSG clear). Within that, the hostname block runs only if there are bytes left (lenMsg > 0) and the caller requested hostname parsing (PARSE_HOSTNAME). The index i is reset to 0 to begin filling bufParseHOSTNAME. The lenMsg > 0 guard here is what protects the subsequent read of p2parse[i] in the loop.

The hostname loop copies characters that are valid in a host name (alphanumeric, '.', '_', '-') from the input into bufParseHOSTNAME. Because && short-circuits left to right, the read p2parse[i] is only reached after i < lenMsg is confirmed, so reads stay within the remaining input, and the write bufParseHOSTNAME[i] executes only after i < (CONF_HOSTNAME_MAXSIZE - 1) is confirmed, so the write index is at most CONF_HOSTNAME_MAXSIZE-2. This leaves index CONF_HOSTNAME_MAXSIZE-1 free for the later NUL terminator. Note the duplicated 'p2parse[i] == '.'' term is a harmless copy-paste typo. The loop cannot overflow bufParseHOSTNAME.

After the copy loop the code decides whether what it collected is really a hostname. If i == lenMsg the message ended exactly at the hostname; it advances the cursor, NUL-terminates bufParseHOSTNAME at index i (<= CONF_HOSTNAME_MAXSIZE-1, in bounds) and records it. Otherwise, if a space follows a run of at least one char whose previous byte is alphanumeric, it treats the run as a hostname, consumes it plus the delimiting space, terminates and records it. In the else-if, i < lenMsg holds (the i==lenMsg branch was not taken), so p2parse[i] and p2parse[i-1] are in-bounds reads. If neither branch matches, no hostname is set and p2parse is left unchanged so the TAG scan re-reads the same bytes.

The TAG scan resets i and copies bytes into bufParseTAG until the input runs out (lenMsg <= 0), a ':' or ' ' terminator is hit, or the buffer is nearly full. The loop condition dereferences *p2parse only after lenMsg > 0 is confirmed, so reads stay in bounds. The capacity guard is i < CONF_TAG_MAXSIZE - 2: the loop stops writing once i reaches CONF_TAG_MAXSIZE-2, meaning the last write is at index CONF_TAG_MAXSIZE-3 and i ends at most CONF_TAG_MAXSIZE-2. Reserving two slots is deliberate -- one for a possible trailing ':' and one for the NUL. This '- 2' slack is precisely the correction that distinguishes the fixed parser from the historically vulnerable one.

If the byte that stopped the loop is a colon (and input remains), the colon is considered part of the TAG: the cursor advances and the colon is appended at bufParseTAG[i++]. Since the loop guaranteed i <= CONF_TAG_MAXSIZE-2 on entry here, this write lands at index at most CONF_TAG_MAXSIZE-2 and leaves i at most CONF_TAG_MAXSIZE-1 -- still one slot for the terminator. This is the first of the two reserved slots being consumed.

what_the_code_does.code:
bufParseTAG[i] = '\0';	/* terminate string */
		MsgSetTAG(pMsg, bufParseTAG, i);
what_the_code_does.explanation: The TAG buffer is NUL-terminated at index i (at most CONF_TAG_MAXSIZE-1, the final valid element) and handed to MsgSetTAG together with the length i. Given the '- 2' loop bound plus at most one colon append, i never exceeds CONF_TAG_MAXSIZE-1, so this terminating write is exactly in-bounds. MsgSetTAG copies i bytes plus the terminator into the message object; its own bound is the i value passed here.

This is the else of the bParseHOSTNAMEandTAG gate: when the operator configured rsyslog to leave the remainder as raw MSG, the parser skips hostname/TAG extraction entirely and only logs a debug note for non-internal messages. p2parse is left pointing at the start of the unparsed text.

Finally the byte offset of the remaining MSG body is recorded by subtracting the original buffer base from the advanced cursor and storing it via MsgSetMSGoffs. This pointer difference is used as an index into pszRawMsg elsewhere, so its correctness depends on p2parse never having advanced past the end of the allocation during parsing. The function then returns 0 (success) unconditionally.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: bufParseTAG is a fixed CONF_TAG_MAXSIZE stack buffer fed by an attacker-controlled, unbounded TAG string. Historically this parser bounded the copy loop too loosely, so a long TAG plus the appended ':' plus the NUL terminator wrote past the end of the stack buffer -- a classic stack buffer overflow (this is the bug this '_fixed' sample corrects, in the CVE-2011-3200 rsyslog family). In the shown code the invariant IS upheld: the loop bound i < CONF_TAG_MAXSIZE - 2 reserves exactly two trailing slots, so after at most one colon append (i -> CONF_TAG_MAXSIZE-1) the terminator write at index i stays inside the buffer. The residual risk is entirely in the macro contract: safety depends on CONF_TAG_MAXSIZE being >= 3 and on the two-slot reservation never being widened again. What must hold: (bytes copied) + (optional 1 colon) + (1 NUL) <= CONF_TAG_MAXSIZE.

what_could_go_wrong.code: } else if(*p2parse == ' ' && lenMsg > 1) { /* try to see if it is slighly malformed - HP procurve seems to do that sometimes */
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: The predicate reads *p2parse as the FIRST operand of the &&, so the byte at the cursor is dereferenced BEFORE lenMsg > 1 is checked. This branch is reached only when both timestamp parsers failed. If the message text after the PRI is empty (lenMsg == 0), or if offAfterPRI was computed >= iLenRawMsg so lenMsg <= 0 (see the line 13 finding), p2parse points at or just past the last content byte and *p2parse is an out-of-bounds read of one byte past the parseable region. The order should be lenMsg > 0 (or > 1) checked FIRST. Whether this is a true OOB depends on whether pszRawMsg is guaranteed to carry a trailing NUL inside its allocation; if it is not, this is a remotely triggerable one-byte over-read (crash / minor info leak). Invariant: never dereference p2parse unless lenMsg > 0 has already been confirmed.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
lenMsg = pMsg->iLenRawMsg - pMsg->offAfterPRI; /* note: offAfterPRI is already the number of PRI chars (do not add one!) */
	p2parse = pMsg->pszRawMsg + pMsg->offAfterPRI; /* point to start of text, after PRI */
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: lenMsg and the cursor base are both derived by subtracting/adding offAfterPRI, with no check that offAfterPRI <= iLenRawMsg. If a malformed PRI scan produced offAfterPRI > iLenRawMsg, lenMsg becomes negative and p2parse points beyond the message content. Because lenMsg is a signed int, this does not wrap to a huge positive length (so the length-guarded loops correctly do nothing), but it feeds the unguarded dereference at line 26 and the pointer subtraction at line 129, where p2parse - pszRawMsg would encode an offset past the buffer. Invariant: the caller must guarantee 0 <= offAfterPRI <= iLenRawMsg; this function should defensively clamp/reject lenMsg <= 0 before any dereference.

bufParseHOSTNAME is the second fixed-size stack buffer fed by attacker input, so it carries the same length-vs-buffer contract as the TAG buffer. Here the copy is correctly bounded: the write executes only when i < (CONF_HOSTNAME_MAXSIZE - 1), and the subsequent terminator write at bufParseHOSTNAME[i] (lines 80/86) lands at index <= CONF_HOSTNAME_MAXSIZE-1, the last valid element. Reads of p2parse[i] are guarded by the leading i < lenMsg. The residual concern is contractual, mirroring the TAG buffer: correctness depends on CONF_HOSTNAME_MAXSIZE >= 1 and on the '- 1' reservation being preserved. No overflow is present in the shown code.

These external parsers receive &p2parse and &lenMsg and are trusted to advance the cursor and decrement the remaining length CONSISTENTLY -- i.e. to move p2parse forward by exactly the same number of bytes they subtract from lenMsg, and never past the buffer end. This function performs no re-validation of p2parse/lenMsg after the callbacks return. If either parser advances the pointer more than it reduces lenMsg (or vice versa) on a crafted timestamp, every downstream bound (line 26 read, hostname/TAG loops, and the final offset) is skewed. Invariant to confirm: ParseTIMESTAMP3339/3164 keep p2parse and lenMsg in lockstep and never overrun.

The MSG offset is the distance the cursor has traveled from the buffer base and is later used to index pszRawMsg for the message body. It is only correct if every preceding step kept p2parse within [pszRawMsg, pszRawMsg + iLenRawMsg]. Given a negative lenMsg (line 13) or a timestamp parser that over-advanced (line 22), p2parse could point past the buffer, making this offset larger than iLenRawMsg and causing out-of-bounds reads wherever the stored MSG offset is later consumed. Invariant: p2parse - pszRawMsg must be within [0, iLenRawMsg].

summary: This is the security-corrected rsyslog legacy syslog parser: the TAG and HOSTNAME copy loops are now correctly bounded (i < CONF_TAG_MAXSIZE - 2 and i < CONF_HOSTNAME_MAXSIZE - 1), closing the historical stack buffer overflow (CVE-2011-3200 family). No memory-corruption write bug is present in the shown code. The remaining, lower-severity concern is a one-byte out-of-bounds READ at line 26, where *p2parse is dereferenced before lenMsg is confirmed positive, reachable when the message is empty after the PRI or when offAfterPRI exceeds iLenRawMsg (line 13 gives no clamp).

reviewed_anchors.reason: Entry signature; pMsg carries the untrusted raw message but the signature itself is just the trust-boundary entry, no bug.
Cursor into attacker data; dereferenced at line 26 before a length check and used in the final pointer subtraction.
Signed remaining-length derived from a subtraction with no lower-bound clamp; can be <= 0 and feed the unguarded read.
Loop index; its range is bounded by the CONF_*_MAXSIZE guards in each loop, so it introduces no independent contract.
Fixed-size stack buffer paired with an attacker-driven copy length -- mandatory finding; historical overflow site.
Fixed-size stack buffer paired with an attacker-driven copy length -- mandatory finding; bound is correct but contractual.
Size macro that must be >= 3 for the '- 2' reservation plus colon plus NUL to fit; covered by the bufParseTAG finding.
Size macro backing the hostname buffer and the '- 1' loop reservation; covered by the bufParseHOSTNAME finding.
Unclamped signed subtraction; offAfterPRI > iLenRawMsg yields negative length and an out-of-buffer cursor.
Cursor base can point past the allocation if offAfterPRI is out of range; feeds line 26 and line 129.
External parser trusted to keep p2parse/lenMsg in lockstep and in-bounds; no re-validation on return.
Same lockstep-advance contract as the 3339 parser; covered by the timestamp-parser finding.
Dereferences p2parse before confirming lenMsg > 0 -- out-of-bounds read primitive.
Same-object copy with a compile-time sizeof length; not attacker-influenced, no overflow.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: flags: IGNDATE / INTERNAL_MSG / PARSE_HOSTNAME and global bParseHOSTNAMEandTAG (lines 40,52,66,123)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Configuration/behavior gates; they narrow which parsing runs but carry no memory-safety contract themselves.
Write bounded by i < (CONF_HOSTNAME_MAXSIZE - 1) and reads bounded by i < lenMsg; no overflow (contract captured by the buffer anchor).
In the reachable branch i < lenMsg holds, so both reads are in-bounds; terminator index <= CONF_HOSTNAME_MAXSIZE-1.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: TAG copy loop bound i < CONF_TAG_MAXSIZE - 2 (line 105)
reviewed_anchors.reason: This is the corrected bound; central to the TAG overflow contract (captured by the bufParseTAG finding).
Consumes one of the two reserved slots; safe given the loop bound (covered by the bufParseTAG finding).
Lands at index <= CONF_TAG_MAXSIZE-1 given the reservation; in-bounds (covered by the bufParseTAG finding).
Callees receive a validated length i and NUL-terminated buffer; their own bounds are out of scope of this file, contract noted.
Stored offset can exceed iLenRawMsg if p2parse over-advanced; downstream OOB when the offset is later indexed.
p2parse is uchar*, so the argument is a non-negative value; no signed-char UB in the isalnum call.

findings.title: Attacker-controlled TAG copied into fixed CONF_TAG_MAXSIZE stack buffer (historical stack overflow; corrected here)
findings.anchor: uchar bufParseTAG[CONF_TAG_MAXSIZE]; while(... i < CONF_TAG_MAXSIZE - 2) { bufParseTAG[i++] = *p2parse++; } ... bufParseTAG[i++] = ':'; ... bufParseTAG[i] = '\0';
findings.bug_class: Stack buffer overflow (unbounded copy into fixed buffer)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0303_cwe119.c (loop) and implementation (file not provided) for CONF_TAG_MAXSIZE / MsgSetTAG
findings.invariant: The number of TAG bytes copied, plus an optional trailing ':' and the NUL terminator, must never exceed CONF_TAG_MAXSIZE; i.e. the loop must stop with i <= CONF_TAG_MAXSIZE - 2 so the colon and NUL still fit.
findings.failure_mode: A remote syslog message with a very long TAG (no ':' or ' ' terminator for a long run) drives the copy. If the loop bound reserved fewer than two trailing bytes (as in the pre-fix code), appending ':' at line 112 and '\0' at line 118 writes past bufParseTAG on the stack -- remote stack smashing / RCE. In the shown code the bound i < CONF_TAG_MAXSIZE - 2 reserves exactly the two needed slots, so no overflow occurs; the residual risk is that CONF_TAG_MAXSIZE must be >= 3 and the reservation must not be loosened.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm CONF_TAG_MAXSIZE >= 3, that no other path writes bufParseTAG beyond index CONF_TAG_MAXSIZE-1, and that MsgSetTAG treats the passed length i as authoritative and does not itself over-read.

findings.title: Out-of-bounds read: *p2parse dereferenced before confirming remaining length
findings.code: } else if(*p2parse == ' ' && lenMsg > 1) { /* try to see if it is slighly malformed - HP procurve seems to do that sometimes */
findings.invariant: p2parse must not be dereferenced unless lenMsg > 0 has already been established; the length test must be the first operand of the &&.
findings.failure_mode: Reached only when both timestamp parsers fail. If the text after the PRI is empty (lenMsg == 0) or offAfterPRI was mis-scanned so lenMsg <= 0 (line 13 does not clamp), p2parse sits at or past the last content byte and *p2parse reads one byte out of bounds. A remote attacker sends a message that fails both timestamp formats and ends exactly at the PRI; impact is a crash or one-byte over-read, contingent on whether pszRawMsg carries a trailing NUL inside its allocation.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify whether pszRawMsg is guaranteed NUL-terminated within its allocation and whether lenMsg can be 0 or negative here; if so, reorder the condition to test lenMsg first.

findings.title: Unclamped signed length: lenMsg = iLenRawMsg - offAfterPRI may be negative and p2parse may point past the buffer
findings.anchor: lenMsg = pMsg->iLenRawMsg - pMsg->offAfterPRI; p2parse = pMsg->pszRawMsg + pMsg->offAfterPRI;
findings.code:
lenMsg = pMsg->iLenRawMsg - pMsg->offAfterPRI; /* note: offAfterPRI is already the number of PRI chars (do not add one!) */
	p2parse = pMsg->pszRawMsg + pMsg->offAfterPRI; /* point to start of text, after PRI */
findings.bug_class: Improper input validation / signed length miscomputation
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0303_cwe119.c (computation); implementation (file not provided) for the PRI scan that sets offAfterPRI
findings.invariant: 0 <= offAfterPRI <= iLenRawMsg must hold so that lenMsg >= 0 and p2parse stays inside [pszRawMsg, pszRawMsg + iLenRawMsg].
findings.failure_mode: If an earlier stage produced offAfterPRI > iLenRawMsg, lenMsg is negative (not a wrap, since it is signed int) and p2parse points beyond the message. The length-guarded loops then no-op, but the unguarded read at line 26 and the pointer difference at line 129 operate on an out-of-buffer cursor, yielding an over-read or a bogus MSG offset consumed downstream. No defensive clamp of lenMsg <= 0 exists in this function.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the PRI parser cannot set offAfterPRI beyond iLenRawMsg, or add an early return when lenMsg <= 0 before any dereference.

findings.title: Attacker-controlled HOSTNAME copied into fixed CONF_HOSTNAME_MAXSIZE stack buffer (bounded, contractual)
findings.anchor: uchar bufParseHOSTNAME[CONF_HOSTNAME_MAXSIZE]; while(... && i < (CONF_HOSTNAME_MAXSIZE - 1)) { bufParseHOSTNAME[i] = p2parse[i]; ... }
findings.bug_class: Fixed-buffer copy bounded by a length guard (no overflow present)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0303_cwe119.c; implementation (file not provided) for CONF_HOSTNAME_MAXSIZE / MsgSetHOSTNAME
findings.invariant: Hostname bytes copied plus the NUL terminator must fit in CONF_HOSTNAME_MAXSIZE; the write index must stay <= CONF_HOSTNAME_MAXSIZE - 1.
findings.failure_mode: A long hostname run of valid characters drives the copy, but the loop stops writing once i reaches CONF_HOSTNAME_MAXSIZE - 1, and the terminator at bufParseHOSTNAME[i] (lines 80/86) lands at the last valid index, so there is no overflow in the shown code. Safety is contingent on CONF_HOSTNAME_MAXSIZE >= 1 and on the '- 1' reservation being preserved; flagged per the fixed-buffer-plus-length rule.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm CONF_HOSTNAME_MAXSIZE is large enough and that MsgSetHOSTNAME honors the passed length i and does not re-scan for a NUL beyond it.

findings.title: Timestamp parsers trusted to keep p2parse and lenMsg in-bounds and in lockstep
findings.bug_class: Unvalidated callee mutation of parse cursor/length
findings.invariant: ParseTIMESTAMP3339/3164 must advance p2parse by exactly the number of bytes they subtract from lenMsg, never move p2parse past pszRawMsg + iLenRawMsg, and never leave lenMsg negative.
findings.failure_mode: This function does not re-validate p2parse or lenMsg after the callbacks return. A crafted timestamp that makes a parser advance the pointer more than it decrements lenMsg (or leaves lenMsg inconsistent) skews every subsequent bound -- the line 26 read, the hostname/TAG loops, and the final MSG offset -- potentially into out-of-bounds territory.
findings.what_to_confirm: Audit ParseTIMESTAMP3339 and ParseTIMESTAMP3164 to confirm they maintain the p2parse/lenMsg lockstep invariant and never overrun on malformed input.

audit_checklist: Confirm CONF_TAG_MAXSIZE >= 3 and CONF_HOSTNAME_MAXSIZE >= 1 so the '- 2' and '- 1' loop reservations plus colon and NUL always fit.
Verify pszRawMsg is guaranteed NUL-terminated inside its allocation, and/or reorder line 26 to test lenMsg > 0 before dereferencing *p2parse.
Guarantee (or clamp) 0 <= offAfterPRI <= iLenRawMsg so lenMsg at line 13 cannot go negative and p2parse cannot start past the buffer.
Audit ParseTIMESTAMP3339/ParseTIMESTAMP3164 to confirm they keep p2parse and lenMsg in lockstep and never advance past the buffer end.
Confirm the MSG offset stored at line 129 (p2parse - pszRawMsg) is always within [0, iLenRawMsg] on every code path, including the timestamp and no-hostname paths.
Check MsgSetTAG / MsgSetHOSTNAME treat the passed length as authoritative and do not re-scan for a terminator beyond it.
